macro_events: CONFIRMED SLUMP — On Mon 15-Jun, LME aluminium settled $3,379.50 (−4.4%), its lowest since 27-Mar, as an interim US-Iran framework deal laid the groundwork to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Aluminium was the worst base-metal performer of the day; copper (+0.3%) and tin (+2.9%) rose — a Hormuz-specific repricing of the war premium, not a complex-wide risk-off.

Deal timeline: the two sides electronically signed a framework on 15-Jun with the formal signing set for Fri 19-Jun (Switzerland); the Strait is to reopen on signing. Trump authorized a 'toll-free opening' of Hormuz, but Iran's free-transit window is reported at only 60 days (Fars), and shipowners say they need more detail before judging safe passage — relief is announced, not yet physically live.

Physical reality check: the deal restores the Gulf logistics path but not the damaged capacity. Middle East ≈10% of global supply with output −35% YoY by April; EGA's Al Taweelah still needs ~12 mo to restore and ALBA remains impaired. A 3–6 mo lag separates the diplomatic resolution from actual metal — the structural deficit persists.

Analyst split frames the two-way risk: BofA (Widmer) sees aluminium 'vulnerable near-term' as supply risks ease, with rising China output, potential Middle East inventory releases and new Indonesian supply as added downside; JPMorgan (Shearer) flags a knee-jerk energy-correlated drop but stresses the 'major supply gap' remains until invisible inventories deplete and visible LME stocks are drawn.

Oil slumped with the premium (Brent ~−4% toward $83; WTI sub-$81) — disinflationary at the margin and supportive of smelting economics — while hot May US inflation (CPI +0.5% m/m, +4.2% y/y; PPI +1.1% m/m) keeps the Fed hawkish and the USD firm (~99.9). China unwrought + semis exports were +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May (5-mo cumulative ~2.69 Mt, +10.4%).
lme_commentary: Commentary: The war premium deflated decisively on 15-Jun — LME aluminium settled $3,379.50 (−4.4%), the lowest since 27-Mar, on the US-Iran interim deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz (formal signing 19-Jun; 60-day transit window). The Westmetall official cash series still reads 12-Jun ($3,536) pending the 15-Jun official print. Crucially the move was aluminium-specific (Cu +0.3%, Sn +2.9% the same day), confirming a Hormuz-premium repricing rather than a broad risk-off. The structural floor remains real: inventories ~320kt (−37% YTD from ~509kt in Jan, ~1.4 days of global output, ~92% Russian-origin), EGA ~12 mo to restore, ALBA impaired, and a 3–6 mo lag before metal physically returns. Net: a lower, more volatile but supported price. Next catalysts — the 19-Jun signing and the actual Strait reopening (shipowners still want detail), plus the USD/Fed path.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: Latest confirmed index; Q2 oversupply pressuring

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$366/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer Platts API region (~$305/t); surplus building on higher production/shipments into subdued demand.

Hormuz reopening (after the 19-Jun signing) reverses the rerouting premium — alumina cargo flows through the Gulf should normalize over coming weeks, easing the logistics distortion that had benefited China.
Bauxite: Guinea's stricter export controls still tighten raw-material supply and add upstream cost pressure — a structural offset independent of the Gulf truce.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply disruption that lifted regional refining costs should ease as the blockade lifts; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery may restart ahead of the smelter.
Net: input-cost picture skews softer post-deal (alumina ample, energy/logistics relief), though Guinea bauxite controls keep a floor under raw-material risk.
net_read: REGIME SHIFT CONFIRMED — WAR PREMIUM DEFLATING, PHYSICAL DEFICIT INTACT. Aluminium's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock; the US-Iran interim deal — electronically signed 15-Jun, formal signing 19-Jun (Switzerland), Hormuz to reopen on signing under a reported 60-day transit window — is unwinding that premium. The market's verdict on 15-Jun was unambiguous: aluminium settled $3,379.50 (−4.4%), a two-month low and the worst base-metal performer of the day, while copper and tin rose. But the structural floor is real and both desks agree the supply gap persists: BofA sees near-term downside (China output, Middle East inventory releases, Indonesian supply) while JPMorgan stresses invisible inventories must deplete before visible LME stocks draw. EGA needs ~12 mo to restore, ALBA is impaired, the Strait physically reopens only on the 19-Jun signing (shipowners still seeking detail), LME stocks keep drawing (~320kt, ~1.4 days, ~92% Russian-origin), and a firm USD (hot May CPI +4.2% y/y) caps rallies. Expect a lower, more volatile but supported price. Watch: the 15-Jun official LME print, the 19-Jun signing and actual Strait reopening, EGA/ALBA restart timing, China export pace, and the USD/Fed path.

feed.text: Aluminium holds near the 15-Jun two-month-low (~$3,380) with no confirmed rebound; the market digests the war-premium unwind ahead of Fri's formal signing. Structural-deficit arguments (BofA/JPM both cite an intact supply gap) cushion but do not reverse the near-term bearish bias.

LME aluminium settled $3,379.50 (−4.4%), lowest since 27-Mar, as the US-Iran interim deal set up a Hormuz reopening on Fri's signing. Aluminium was the day's worst base metal; copper +0.3%, tin +2.9% — a Hormuz-specific repricing (Bloomberg/LME).

Trump authorized a 'toll-free opening' of Hormuz; Iran to allow free transit for only 60 days (Fars). Shipowners say they need more detail before judging safe passage — logistics relief announced, not yet physically live.
BofA (Widmer): aluminium 'vulnerable near-term' as supply risks ease — rising China output, potential Middle East inventory releases and new Indonesian supply are compounding downside risks.

JPMorgan (Shearer): expect a knee-jerk, energy-correlated drop, but the 'major supply gap' remains — the key question is how long before invisible inventories are depleted and visible LME stocks start to draw.
Oil slumped with the premium — Brent ~−4% toward $83, WTI sub-$81 — as the Hormuz reopening deflates the risk premium; disinflationary at the margin and supportive of smelting economics.
Pre-deal, LME official cash settled $3,536 (+1.1% d/d), 3M $3,532 — last confirmed Westmetall official; the 15-Jun official cash print is still pending.
LME inventory drew to 319,925 t (−2,075 d/d), down ~37% YTD; ~92% Russian-origin keeps available units scarce — the deficit is unaffected by the deal.

news.headline: LME aluminium settled $3,379.50 (−4.4%) on 15-Jun, a two-month low (lowest since 27-Mar) and the worst base-metal performer of the day, as the war premium deflated on the US-Iran deal.

news.source: NPR / PBS / Al Jazeera / Bloomberg (14–15 Jun)

news.headline: Deal restores Gulf logistics but not capacity: Middle East ~10% of global supply, output −35% YoY by April. EGA ~12 mo to restore Al Taweelah; ALBA impaired — structural deficit intact.

BofA flags aluminium 'vulnerable near-term' (China output, ME inventory release, Indonesian supply); JPMorgan warns of a knee-jerk drop but says the major supply gap persists until invisible inventories deplete.
Cash-3M backwardation collapsed toward flat from 19-yr highs (~$69 on 08-Jun) as front-end war-premium scarcity eased, though total stocks stay low.
LME stocks ~320kt (−37% YTD), ~1.4 days of global output; ~92% Russian-origin. Deficit unaffected by the deal; visible draws yet to begin in earnest.
Oil fell ~4% post-deal (Brent toward $83) as Hormuz reopening deflates the risk premium; disinflationary at the margin, though hot May CPI keeps the Fed hawkish and USD firm.
China unwrought Al + semis exports +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May (5-mo ~2.69 Mt, +10.4%); BofA flags rising Indonesian smelter output as an added near-term bearish variable.

commercial.text: The 15-Jun −4.4% slump to $3,379.50 confirms the war premium is unwinding faster than the rebound thesis implied — but the unrepaired Gulf capacity (EGA ~12 mo) and low LME stocks cap the downside. Accelerate near-dated realization and ensure LME hedges are in place on priced tonnes; do NOT chase a top that has already broken, and do NOT over-hedge forward at distressed levels given the intact structural deficit. Maximise value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture while elevated.

The Gulf outage drove MJP Q2 +79.5% q/q (~$350–353) and record US MW premiums; the Hormuz reopening (post the 19-Jun signing) will normalize that support over coming weeks. Lock in elevated premiums on renewals and spot allocations NOW, ahead of the 3–6 mo physical-tightness lag giving way to normalized flows. As a non-disrupted supplier, capture premium share while EGA/ALBA remain impaired.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$366/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer API region (~$305/t); the deal reverses the Hormuz rerouting premium. If net seller, lock firm ex-China pricing on remaining strength; if buyer for smelting, exploit softer spot windows as Gulf flows normalize.

Hormuz reopening and blockade removal should lower Gulf freight, war-risk insurance and routing risk — but the Strait physically reopens only on the 19-Jun signing and shipowners still want detail, so relief is not yet live. Keep contingency routing and demurrage clauses live through the 60-day window until normalization is confirmed.

Manage the transition narrative with strategic accounts: physical tightness persists through the lag even as the geopolitical risk recedes and headline prices fall. Protect OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and convert reliability through the volatility into relationship equity and premium tolerance.

Track: (1) the 15-Jun official LME cash print and the scale of the premium unwind; (2) deal implementation — 19-Jun signing, the actual Strait reopening, shipowner safe-passage confirmation, 60-day window durability; (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines; (4) USD/Fed path (hot May CPI, Dec decision); (5) China export pace, Indonesian smelter ramp & Section 232 tariffs; (6) Guinea bauxite / alumina balance.

bottom_line: A regime shift, now confirmed by price: the 15-Jun −4.4% slump to $3,379.50 (a two-month low) is the war premium deflating on the US-Iran deal (formal signing 19-Jun, 60-day Hormuz window). Expect a lower, choppier price — but the unrepaired Gulf capacity, drawing LME stocks and a 3–6 month supply lag keep a structural floor, and both BofA and JPMorgan agree the supply gap persists even as near-term risks skew down. For a reliable Gulf-adjacent producer: lock in elevated premiums and near-dated realization before flows normalize, hedge priced tonnes through the volatility, and avoid over-committing forward at distressed levels.
sources: London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (LME_Al_cash table, 12-Jun official cash; 15-Jun cash pending)

Bank of America (Michael Widmer) & JPMorgan (Gregory Shearer) via Bloomberg/Mining.com
US Bureau of Labor Statistics (CPI released 10-Jun-26)
S&P Global Platts assessments (Dec-25 / Jan-26 / Feb-26; MJP Q2)
caveats: The US-Iran deal was electronically signed 15-Jun but the formal MOU signing is set for 19-Jun; the Strait of Hormuz reopens for commercial shipping only on signing, Iran's free-transit window is reported at 60 days, and shipowners still want detail before judging safe passage — logistics relief is announced but not yet physically live, and damaged Gulf capacity is unrepaired.

The 15-Jun figure of $3,379.50 is the confirmed LME benchmark settlement (Bloomberg/LME). The Westmetall official-cash series still reads 12-Jun ($3,536) pending the 15-Jun official print; the 15-Jun row in the price/stock series uses the confirmed settle with an estimated cash (≈3M, reflecting backwardation collapse) and an estimated stock (trend-extrapolated) — reconcile to Westmetall once published.

Macro/FX/energy values are the latest confirmed reads and are point-in-time; oil (Brent/WTI) is reconciled to the confirmed post-deal direction (down ~4%) and specific levels should be re-validated against live feeds before use.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus). Values shown are most recent public anchors / status — reconcile to the desk's licensed feed before pricing.
SHFE front-month is a last-reported reference (~CNY 24,000–24,400/t SMM spot, excl-VAT USD equivalent); verify on SMM / SHFE for the live print.
Alumina indices diverge by methodology (Fastmarkets FOB Australia vs Platts API region) — use the index contractually relevant to the trade.
This report is market intelligence, not trading or investment advice. Figures are point-in-time and should be re-validated before commercial commitments.

China June trade & aluminium export data

ai_analysis: What changed today: the 15-Jun official settlement confirmed a 4.4% slump to $3,379.50 (a two-month low) — a bigger, cleaner break than the prior 'rebound to ~$3,550' read; the war-premium unwind is now the dominant near-term driver.

Aluminium-specific move: copper (+0.3%) and tin (+2.9%) rose the same day, so this is a Hormuz-premium repricing, not a complex-wide risk-off — the deficit narrative is intact, the geopolitical bid is not.

Term structure: the cash–3M backwardation collapsed from 19-yr highs (~$69 on 08-Jun) toward flat — the clearest signal that acute front-end scarcity is easing, even as total LME stocks keep drawing (~320kt, −37% YTD).

Sentiment skew: news flow tilts net-bearish near-term (premium unwind, oil −4%, BofA caution, China/Indonesia supply) but the structural floor (JPM 'major supply gap') limits downside — a decisive tilt likely around the 19-Jun signing and the first real Strait transits.

Forward bias: base case (50%) = a lower, choppier but supported price ($3,300–3,600 for 2026); main upside risk is a slipped EGA/ALBA restart, main downside is fast Gulf normalization plus China/Indonesia supply.

exec_summary: Aluminum's de-escalation sell-off accelerated. LME cash settled at $3,379.50/t on Monday 15 June, down 4.4% on the day to a two-month low (the lowest since 27 March) and now roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855 — the single worst performer across the base-metals complex (copper +0.3%, tin +2.9%). A parallel ~4% drop in Brent (toward ~$83/bbl) as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated compounded the bearish signal for the most energy-intensive base metal, with a firmer dollar (DXY near 100) a secondary headwind. Even after the correction, aluminum holds about +20% year-to-date and ~+45% year-on-year.

The driver is the US-Iran framework. Trump and Vice President Vance digitally signed a memorandum of understanding for a 60-day all-fronts ceasefire that lifts the US naval blockade and reopens the Strait of Hormuz “permanently toll-free,” with formal signing in Geneva on Friday 19 June and ships reportedly beginning to move. The prompt-scarcity premium is collapsing — the cash-to-3M backwardation has deflated from +$69/t (8 June, a ~19-year high) toward flat. Physical tightness has not yet released, however: LME stocks sit at multi-year lows (~320 kt, ~1.4 days of global output) and are still drawing, the Gulf restart is slow (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline, regional output -35% YoY in April), and invisible inventories deplete before visible tightness eases. Net read: the war-risk premium is unwinding fast, but the deal's 60-day conditionality and a months-long physical restart leave a two-sided market — near-term bearish, with sharp-rebound risk if the framework slips.

price_table.note: Latest 15 Jun; 2-mo low (lowest since 27 Mar); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
Tracks cash; 15 Jun est. (official pending) - curve flattening toward contango
War-risk backwardation deflated from +$69 (8 Jun, ~19-yr high); contango risk as scarcity eases
12 Jun official; multi-yr lows ~1.4 days output; still drawing - physical tightness persists
Q2 oversupply persists; oil-led energy relief adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (decade high); Q3 talks open >=$350 but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran MOU signed (digitally by Trump and VP Vance) for a 60-day all-fronts ceasefire - lifts the US naval blockade and reopens the Strait of Hormuz “permanently toll-free,” with formal signing in Geneva on Friday 19 June and ships reportedly beginning to move. This de-escalation is the dominant catalyst unwinding the conflict premium.

Aluminum was the worst base-metals performer on 15 June (-4.4% to $3,379.50, a 2-month low) versus copper +0.3% and tin +2.9% - reflecting its concentrated Hormuz and energy exposure; the metal is now ~12% off the 2 June four-year high of $3,855.

Energy de-risking compounds the bear case: Brent fell ~4% (toward ~$83/bbl) as the war premium deflated; lower power and gas costs pull down the smelter cost floor - directly bearish for the most electricity-intensive (13-15 MWh/t) base metal.

Physical market still tight: LME stocks at multi-year lows (~320 kt, ~1.4 days output) and still drawing; Gulf recovery is slow (EGA ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline, regional output -35% YoY in April), and invisible inventories deplete before visible tightness eases (Shearer/JPM) - risk of premature price optimism.

Compounding supply: China May exports rose to 632 kt but output nears the 45 Mt cap (a finite buffer - BofA/Widmer), Indonesian smelter ramp adds new tonnes, and Guinea bauxite controls are a modest upstream offset. The 60-day conditionality and shipowner caution are the swing factors on how fast Gulf supply actually returns.

implications.text: the de-escalation has flipped the near-term tape decisively lower and the curve is flattening toward contango, so the urgency to sell prompt-only is gone and a window is opening to layer forward hedges on priced tonnes - but avoid chasing forward fixed-price sales into a falling, headline-driven market. Protect value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA) and keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the framework slip on the 60-day conditionality.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 ~$351.5/t, a decade high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) and Q3 MJP talks are opening at or above $350/t, but a confirmed Hormuz reopening is now the principal downside risk to premiums into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 settlements and renewal terms before normalisation feeds through.

softer ex-China alumina (~$300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; a reopened Strait lowers freight and war-risk insurance - begin unwinding contingency-routing premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure, but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe Hormuz passage (caution explicitly flagged).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the 19 June Geneva signing, the credibility and mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit confirmations, EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: 19 June Geneva signing - whether it holds and how the 60-day ceasefire and any renewal mechanism are framed; slippage or collapse is the primary upside-price risk.
Hormuz transit confirmations - whether shipowners validate sustained safe passage; physical supply returns in months, not weeks, with damaged-smelter restarts gating the pace.
LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve - continued draws and any re-steepening would signal the deficit persists despite the peace narrative, while a flip to contango confirms the unwind.
Compounding bears versus structural deficit - China output against the 45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, the visible/invisible inventory gap, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the USD.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (through 12 June); 15 June LME settlement (-4.4% to $3,379.50, 2-month low) via New Straits Times/Reuters and Discovery Alert; benchmark price, USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran framework / MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening and 19 June Geneva signing via CNN, NBC News, NPR, ABC News and Al Jazeera (14-15 June); inventory and scenario commentary citing JPMorgan (Shearer) and Bank of America (Widmer) via Discovery Alert; China May export data via prior GAC customs reporting; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts; regional premiums (MJP/CIF Japan, US Midwest) via S&P Global Platts and Fastmarkets.
weekly.caveat: Note: 15 June 3-month, SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
